seoTitle: Add a Caption to an Image in Google Docs Cleanly
metaDescription: Learn three ways to caption images in Google Docs: inline text wrapping, the Drawing Canvas, and a borderless table that locks the label in place.
intro: Google Docs has no built-in caption tool, but there are a few reliable workarounds to keep a description locked to its image even as your document grows. This guide covers the quick inline method, the more permanent Drawing Canvas approach, and a borderless table technique for maximum stability.

steps.heading: Insert your image and set text wrapping
steps.body: Upload your image and adjust its alignment using the text-wrapping toolbar options.

steps.heading: Type a caption directly beneath it
steps.body: For a quick label, type your caption text right under an image formatted with Wrap Text.

steps.heading: Or anchor the label with the Drawing Canvas
steps.body: Use the Drawing tool to permanently attach a text label to the graphic itself, so they never separate.

steps.heading: Or use a borderless single-column table
steps.body: Place the image and caption inside a table, then remove the grid borders for a clean, locked layout.

steps.heading: Style your caption text
steps.body: Set the caption font to around nine or ten points and apply italics for a professional, scannable look.

faq.question: Does Google Docs have a native caption feature?
faq.answer: No, there's no built-in captioning tool, but inline text wrapping, the Drawing Canvas, or a borderless table can all achieve the same effect.

faq.question: Why does my caption move away from the image when I add text?
faq.answer: Simple inline captions can shift with surrounding paragraphs; using the Drawing Canvas or a borderless table keeps the caption locked to the image.

faq.question: What font size should I use for captions?
faq.answer: Nine or ten point italic text is a common, professional-looking choice for image captions.

faq.question: Will a borderless table look like a regular table?
faq.answer: No, removing the grid borders makes it invisible, so it just looks like the image and caption sitting together.
